text: The esophagogastroscopy reveals insufficiency of the cardia, dehiscence of the sphincter, and a visible polyp located at the cardia.
The gastroscope is inserted, revealing the cardia gaping and failing to cover the tube, indicating insufficiency.

The surgeon utilizes five laparoscopic ports to perform a standard fundoplication procedure. A ten-millimeter port is used for optics, while five-millimeter ports are positioned under the sternum for a liver retractor and three additional five-millimeter ports serve as working ports.
The surgeon inserts a Natanson liver retractor into the abdominal cavity, revealing adhesions between the left lobe of the liver and the surrounding mantle.
The surgeon inserts three laparoscopic ports into the abdominal cavity: one with the left hand, one with the right hand, and one by the assistant.
The surgeon performs gasolysis using harmonic scissors with ultrasound energy to achieve rare retraction of the liver, enhancing exposure of the operation field.
The surgeon removes the stomach and cardia from the chest cavity, repositioning them into the abdominal cavity. The assistant then fixes the fundus securely within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies the pars flaccida and divides it in a vascular zone using harmonic scissors, with visible separation of tissue in the surgical field.
The hepatic branch of the vagus nerve is visible, with the right crux serving as a landmark. The surgeon approaches the right crux for further dissection.
The surgeon visualizes the right crux and proceeds to move along the anterior semicircle, gradually transitioning to the left crux.
The surgeon locates the cleavage plane between the right crus and the esophagus, using dissection techniques to navigate from the right to the left crus.
Using blunt dissection, the surgeon divides the tissue over the esophagus, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the phrenoesophageal membrane or ligament and proceeds towards the left crus, ensuring to remain within the established cleavage plane.

The surgeon navigates the layer between the esophagus and the right and left crura, revealing the posterior mediastinum. Careful dissection is performed near the left crus to avoid the posterior vagus nerve, which is often drawn into this area.
The surgeon mobilizes the fundus of the stomach for fundoplication, carefully working with ultrasound in coagulation mode to manage the anterior and posterior short gastric vessels.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the fundus of the stomach, focusing on the posterior wall and left crux. Attention is given to the posterior gastric artery, which may be present in this area. The remnants of the frontal fundus ligament are divided following the mobilization.
The surgeon uses a hook instrument to assist in the dissection around the esophagus, focusing on maintaining the cleavage plane near the left crux.

The surgeon divides the tissue fixing the stomach to the chest, aiming to expose the clear left pleura. The dissection proceeds into the cleavage plane between the left cruse and esophagus, entering the retroesophageal space.
The surgeon views the fundus of the stomach through the surgical window and divides the remnants of the geomembran tissue using surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses an esophageal retractor to divide the remnants of the retroesophageal membrane, known as the membrane of Lyman-Bortelli, while carefully avoiding the posterior vagus nerve.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the esophagus, revealing the anterior vagus nerve. The dissection progresses into the cleavage plane between the esophagus and the left crux, extending into the posterior mediastinum. Blind dissection is used to separate remnants of the front esophageal ligament.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection to divide the phrenogastric ligament and immobilize the esophagus. The fundus is visible through the surgical window, and the surgeon carefully divides tissue in the posterior part of the esophagus, taking small portions at a time.

The surgeon takes the fundus and performs the shoe shine maneuver, ensuring a tension-free fundoplication. The esophagus is further mobilized, with careful use of cautery near the esophagus to expose small portions. The left side of the esophagus is completely mobilized, with the abdominal part measuring two to three centimeters.
The surgeon begins posterior crurography using 2-0 Ethibond sutures, focusing on the posterior aspect of the diaphragm's crura.

The surgeon carefully situates the left crura, taking precautions to avoid damaging the nearby aorta. Similarly, while positioning the right crura, the surgeon is cautious to prevent harm to the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon uses an octagonal stitch for posterior crurography, overlapping the sutures to bring the crura closer together without tying the knots too tightly on the diaphragm to prevent ischemia and atrophy of the crura.

The surgeon observes a decrease in the esophageal orifice size and performs anterior crurography, taking care to avoid placing excessive sutures behind the esophagus to prevent altering its entry angle into the abdominal cavity, which could cause dysphagia.
The surgeon performs a fundoplication using the Nissen rosette short floppy technique, placing two or three interrupted sutures with Etibond II to secure the wrap around the esophagus.
The surgeon applies a second interrupted suture during the fundoplication procedure, using Etibond II to secure the tissue.
The surgeon places the first suture between the stomach wall and the remnants of the frontal esophageal ligament, serving as a fixing suture to prevent slippage syndrome.

The final view of the operation shows a short, floppy Nissen fundoplication performed without tension. The elongated abdominal part of the esophagus is visible, along with the completed posterior and anterior crurography.
